subject: ap-chemistry
title: Aluminum Chemistry: Atomic Structure, Solution Prep & Electrochemistry
stimulus: Answer the following questions relating to the element aluminum, Al.

parts.prompt: Write the complete ground-state electron configuration of an Al atom.
parts.scoring_notes: Aluminum has atomic number 13. Ground-state electron configuration: $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^1$. Also accept noble-gas shorthand: $[\text{Ne}] 3s^2 3p^1$.

scoring_points.description: For a correct complete ground-state electron configuration: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^1$

Al has 13 electrons. Fill orbitals in order: $1s^2$ (2 e$^-$), $2s^2$ (2), $2p^6$ (6), $3s^2$ (2), $3p^1$ (1) = 13 total.

wrong_examples: Writing $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^2$ — Al has 13 electrons, not 14 (that would be Si).
Writing $1s^2 2s^2 2p^5 3s^2 3p^2$ — the $2p$ subshell fills to $2p^6$ before $3s$.
common_traps: Al is element 13. Aufbau order: 1s, 2s, 2p, 3s, 3p. Count electrons carefully: 2+2+6+2+1 = 13.

parts.prompt: Based on principles of atomic structure, explain why the radius of the Al atom is larger than the radius of the $\text{Al}^{3+}$ ion.
parts.scoring_notes: When Al loses 3 electrons to form $\text{Al}^{3+}$, the entire n=3 shell is removed (configuration becomes $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6$, same as Ne). The electrons in the $\text{Al}^{3+}$ ion occupy only up to n=2, which is at a smaller average distance from the nucleus than n=3. With the same nuclear charge (+13) but fewer electrons (no n=3 electrons) and less electron-electron repulsion, the remaining electrons are pulled closer to the nucleus. Therefore $\text{Al}^{3+}$ is much smaller than Al.

scoring_points.description: For a correct explanation citing loss of the outermost electron shell upon ionization: 1 point

required_elements: Forming $\text{Al}^{3+}$ removes all n=3 electrons, leaving only n=1 and n=2 electrons.
The remaining electrons are closer to the nucleus, giving $\text{Al}^{3+}$ a smaller radius.
alternatives.correct_example: The Al atom has electrons in the $n = 3$ shell ($3s^2 3p^1$). When three electrons are removed to form $\text{Al}^{3+}$, the entire $n = 3$ shell is emptied. The outermost electrons in $\text{Al}^{3+}$ are now in the $n = 2$ shell, which is much closer to the nucleus. Additionally, with fewer electrons, electron-electron repulsion is reduced, drawing the remaining electrons even closer. Therefore, the $\text{Al}^{3+}$ ion has a smaller radius than the Al atom.

wrong_examples: Saying the nuclear charge increases upon ionization — the number of protons (+13) does not change.
Citing increased effective nuclear charge without noting the loss of the n=3 shell — the primary reason is the complete removal of the outermost principal quantum level.
common_traps: Al → $\text{Al}^{3+}$: loses $3s^2 3p^1$ (the entire n=3 shell). The ion's outermost electrons are in n=2 (much smaller radius). Same nuclear charge but fewer electron shells = smaller radius.

parts.prompt:
In the following table, briefly list the steps necessary to prepare 200.0 mL of an aqueous solution of $\text{AgNO}_3$ using only equipment selected from the choices given. Assume that all appropriate safety measures are already in place. Not all equipment or lines in the table may be needed.

Available: Solid $\text{AgNO}_3$, weighing paper and scoop, 250 mL beakers, distilled water, 200.00 mL volumetric flask, pipet, balance, 50.0 mL graduated cylinder.

Step 1 is given: Use weighing paper to measure the determined mass of solid $\text{AgNO}_3$ on a balance.
parts.scoring_notes: After weighing (Step 1), the procedure must: (i) dissolve the solid in distilled water in the volumetric flask with partial filling, swirling, and addition of more water to the calibration mark; (ii) use the 200.00 mL volumetric flask (not a graduated cylinder) to achieve the precise 200.0 mL volume.

scoring_points.description: For correct steps to dissolve the solute in water (transfer to flask, add water, dissolve): 1 point

required_elements: Transfer weighed $\text{AgNO}_3$ to the 200.00 mL volumetric flask.
Add some distilled water and swirl/mix to dissolve the solid.
alternatives.correct_example:
Step 2: Partially fill the 200.00 mL volumetric flask with distilled water.
Step 3: Transfer the weighed $\text{AgNO}_3$ to the volumetric flask.
Step 4: Swirl the flask to dissolve the solid.

wrong_examples: Using a beaker or graduated cylinder for the final volume — the volumetric flask provides the precise 200.0 mL volume.
Dissolving in a beaker and pouring into the flask without rinsing — quantitative transfer requires rinsing.
common_traps: The 200.00 mL volumetric flask is the only piece of equipment that measures exactly 200.0 mL. Dissolve in partial water first; then dilute to the calibration mark.

scoring_points.description: For the correct final step to reach exactly 200.0 mL (dilute to the calibration mark): 1 point

required_elements: After dissolving, add distilled water to the 200.00 mL calibration mark on the volumetric flask and mix/invert.
alternatives.correct_example: Step 5: Add distilled water dropwise until the bottom of the meniscus is at the 200.00 mL calibration mark. Stopper and invert the flask several times to mix.

wrong_examples: Stopping at a volume other than 200.00 mL — must fill to the exact graduation mark.
Adding all water at once instead of dropwise near the mark — overshoot is possible if not careful near the mark.
common_traps: Final step must explicitly state filling to the calibration mark of the volumetric flask. The volumetric flask (not graduated cylinder) gives the precise volume. Mix after sealing.

parts.prompt:
The following diagram gives an incomplete particulate representation of the reaction. The beaker on the left represents the system before the mixture reacts. Complete the drawing on the right to represent the system after the reaction has occurred. Be sure to include 1) the correct type and number of particles based on the number shown on the left and 2) the relative spacing to depict the appropriate phases.

$\text{Al}(s) + 3\text{Ag}^+(aq) \rightarrow \text{Al}^{3+}(aq) + 3\text{Ag}(s)$

| Half-Reaction | $E^\circ$ |
|---|---|
| $\text{Ag}^+(aq) + e^- \rightarrow \text{Ag}(s)$ | $+0.80$ V |
| $\text{Al}^{3+}(aq) + 3e^- \rightarrow \text{Al}(s)$ | $-1.66$ V |
parts.scoring_notes: The after-reaction drawing must show: (1) Conservation of matter: all Ag originally as $\text{Ag}^+$ ions appear as Ag(s) solid deposits; Al(s) disappears and $\text{Al}^{3+}$ ions appear in solution; (2) Conservation of charge: net ionic charge is preserved (same total ionic charge in solution); (3) Correct phases: Ag(s) shown as closely-packed solid particles near the container walls or bottom; $\text{Al}^{3+}$ shown as dispersed ions in solution; any excess Al(s) as solid if present.

parts.prompt: Using the standard reduction potentials, calculate the value of $E^\circ$ for the reaction.
parts.scoring_notes: $E^\circ_{cell} = E^\circ_{cathode} - E^\circ_{anode}$. Cathode (reduction): $\text{Ag}^+ + e^- \rightarrow \text{Ag}$, $E^\circ = +0.80$ V. Anode (oxidation): $\text{Al} \rightarrow \text{Al}^{3+} + 3e^-$, $E^\circ_{anode} = -1.66$ V. $E^\circ_{cell} = 0.80 - (-1.66) = 0.80 + 1.66 = +2.46$ V.

scoring_points.description: For the correct calculated $E^\circ_{cell} = +2.46$ V: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example:
$E^\circ_{cell} = E^\circ_{cathode} - E^\circ_{anode} = (+0.80\text{ V}) - (-1.66\text{ V}) = +2.46\text{ V}$

Cathode: $\text{Ag}^+$ is reduced (gains electrons). Anode: Al is oxidized (loses electrons). Ag has the higher (more positive) reduction potential, so it is the cathode.

wrong_examples: Adding the two reduction potentials: $0.80 + (-1.66) = -0.86$ V — must subtract $E^\circ_{anode}$, not add it.
Reversing cathode and anode — Al is oxidized (lower reduction potential); Ag is reduced (higher reduction potential).
common_traps: $E^\circ_{cell} = E^\circ_{cathode} - E^\circ_{anode}$ (both as reduction potentials). The species with the higher (more positive) reduction potential is reduced at the cathode. Do NOT flip the sign of the anode reduction potential and add — instead subtract directly.

parts.prompt: Based on the value of $E^\circ$, would the standard free energy change of the reaction under standard conditions, $\Delta G^\circ$, be positive, negative, or zero? Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ_{cell}$. Since $E^\circ_{cell} = +2.46$ V > 0, $\Delta G^\circ = -nF(+2.46) < 0$. A negative $\Delta G^\circ$ means the reaction is thermodynamically spontaneous under standard conditions.

scoring_points.description: For 'negative' with correct justification using $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ_{cell}$ and positive $E^\circ$: 1 point

alternatives.correct_example: Negative. Using $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ_{cell}$: since $E^\circ_{cell} = +2.46$ V > 0 and both $n$ and $F$ are positive constants, $\Delta G^\circ$ is negative. A negative $\Delta G^\circ$ indicates the reaction is thermodynamically spontaneous under standard conditions.

wrong_examples: Saying $\Delta G^\circ$ is positive — $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ$; positive $E^\circ$ → negative $\Delta G^\circ$.
Saying $\Delta G^\circ$ is zero — this would require $E^\circ_{cell} = 0$, which corresponds to equilibrium.
common_traps: Key relationship: $\Delta G^\circ = -nFE^\circ_{cell}$. Positive $E^\circ_{cell}$ → negative $\Delta G^\circ$ → spontaneous. Negative $E^\circ_{cell}$ → positive $\Delta G^\circ$ → non-spontaneous. $E^\circ_{cell} = 0$ → $\Delta G^\circ = 0$ → system at equilibrium.

parts.prompt: Once the reaction appears to stop progressing, would the change in free energy, $\Delta G$, be positive, negative, or zero? Justify your answer.
parts.scoring_notes: When the reaction appears to stop progressing, the system has reached equilibrium. At equilibrium, $\Delta G = 0$ and $E_{cell} = 0$. The cell is fully discharged; there is no net driving force for the reaction in either direction.

scoring_points.description: For 'zero' with justification that the reaction has reached equilibrium: 1 point

required_elements: The reaction has reached equilibrium (no net change), where $\Delta G = 0$ and $E_{cell} = 0$.
alternatives.correct_example: Zero. When the reaction stops progressing, the system has reached equilibrium. At equilibrium, there is no net driving force in either direction, so $\Delta G = 0$ (equivalently, $E_{cell} = 0$, meaning the cell is fully discharged with $Q = K$).

wrong_examples: Saying $\Delta G$ is negative because the reaction was spontaneous — $\Delta G^\circ$ < 0 for the standard conditions, but $\Delta G = 0$ at equilibrium.
Saying $\Delta G$ is positive because the reaction stopped — a positive $\Delta G$ would mean the reverse reaction is spontaneous; at equilibrium, $\Delta G = 0$.
common_traps: Distinguish $\Delta G^\circ$ (standard conditions) from $\Delta G$ (actual conditions). At equilibrium: $\Delta G = 0$, $Q = K$, $E_{cell} = 0$. This is the dead battery state.
